TextENGNew: If I was going to be torn apart either way, being torn apart in body would have been far better.
I believed in her. ...No—I believe in her.
changeReason: Changed 'trusted/trust' to 'believed in/believe in'; these carry different nuances — 'believe in' conveys faith, while 'trust' conveys reliance.

TextENGNew: Even right now, in this very moment, I believe in her.
changeReason: Changed 'trust' to 'believe in,' maintaining the thematic shift from reliance to faith.

TextENGNew: But...somewhere deep down, I've already understood. The only reason I want to believe is that I can't bear to admit the truth.
changeReason: Changed from beginning to realize to already understanding; the new version conveys a deeper, more established awareness per the JPN 薄々は気付いてる.

TextENGNew: That voice trying to persuade itself—that teary, pathetic voice—so utterly, unbearably ridiculous...
More tears came. Turning my face into more of a wreck...
That mechanical repetition finally fell quiet, and a great stillness came.
Only the song of the higurashi remained, obnoxiously loud.
changeReason: Changed 'cicadas' to 'higurashi' (preserving the Japanese term) and 'cry' to 'song,' altering the specificity and emotional coloring.

TextENGNew: And yet...I feel as though I can still hear her.
...That's impossible. She's already stopped saying it.
changeReason: Changed 'She is no longer speaking' to 'She's already stopped saying it'; the new version specifies she stopped repeating a particular phrase, per the JPN 言うのをやめている.

TextENGNew: The only one crying was me. She never cried. Even as she repeated those words again and again, there was no expression on her face. No feeling at all.
If she had no tears to shed for me... then I shouldn't need to shed any for her either.
And yet...this ache. My eyes filling up again. Why?
Because I still want to believe...that I haven't been torn apart.
That's enough, right? My inner voice whispered, gently...
I've already put my heart through enough.
...And time and again I'd wondered whether I should just let the whole hurting thing go.
But I... I stubbornly refused to, didn't I?
If I let it go, the pain would ease...
Knowing all that, I still chose to believe, didn't I?
That painful effort is something only I'll ever truly understand, and only I can ever truly honor.
Hey. Me. ...I tried hard enough. ...I'll give myself credit for that.
So. ...Isn't it okay to let yourself off the hook now...?
changeReason: Changed 'take the easy way out' to 'let yourself off the hook'; the former implies giving up, the latter self-forgiveness, altering the emotional framing per JPN 楽になってもいいんじゃないか.

TextENGNew: Besides—I'm not throwing it away. I'm leaving it behind, with her. ...Like laying flowers at a grave.
All right. ...Settle your nerves... Even though your right arm has gone numb by now. ...Lift it up one more time. And with every swing, let go of a little more.
Your kindness made me happy.
changeReason: Changed 'That kindness' to 'Your kindness'; the new version directly addresses the person, matching the more intimate tone of the JPN 親切が.

TextENGNew: That lovely smile made me happy.
I loved patting your head.
changeReason: Changed 'liked' to 'loved' and 'petting' to 'patting'; 'loved' conveys stronger feeling, closer to the intimate 好きだった in context.

TextENGNew: I loved how you'd blush when I did.
changeReason: Changed 'how demure you were' (general trait) to 'how you'd blush when I did' (specific reaction to patting); the new version correctly links to the preceding action per JPN はにかむ.

TextENGNew: Because this is the last time. Because when I swing this down, I'll forget.
This is for you. ...My first and last bouquet.
It could be...that I really did...love you.
...It felt like someone had been apologizing for a long time.
I wonder what she was apologizing for.
It felt wrong to eavesdrop, so I made an effort not to listen.
It had been a while since I'd been back to the city—I only returned for a relative's funeral.
Even though I'd been living there until just last month, the noise and bustle of the city overwhelmed me.
Skyscrapers and roads with more lanes than you could count.
The crossing signals singing their cheerful little tunes.
changeReason: Changed abstract 'melodious cacophony of the crosswalk' to concrete 'crossing signals singing their cheerful little tunes'; per JPN 歌うように騒がしい横断歩道のメロディ, the new version better captures the singing quality.

TextENGNew: Even the election campaign speeches blaring in front of the station felt strangely nostalgic now.
There's nothing like any of that where I live now.
Just the cicadas. The babbling of a clear stream. And the cry of the higurashi at dusk.
changeReason: Old translates セミ as 'locusts' instead of 'cicadas' — different insects. Cicadas are thematically central to Higurashi. Old does mention 'evening cicadas' later in the same line, partially mitigating the error.

TextENGNew: Only recently have I begun to feel peace in that quiet—not loneliness.
It's true that there's nothing where I live now.
Not just no decent burger places—not even vending machines.
No music shops. No restaurants. No arcades.
An ice cream parlor? Out of the question.
The nearest town has things like that, but it's an hour's bike ride away.
Though really, when you stop and think about it, that wasn't such a big hardship.
Sure, there were music shops and arcades and ice cream parlors back home, but it wasn't like I was ever a regular at any of them.
In ten years of living there, I never once set foot inside an ice cream parlor.
...I should have gone at least once.
I can't help regretting that a little, even now.
...She was still apologizing.
changeReason: Changed 'Somebody' to 'She,' revealing the person's gender earlier than the JPN 誰かが ('someone') intends; also shifts tense from present to past.

TextENGNew: Who was she apologizing to?
She'd apologized this much already—surely whoever it was should just forgive her.
Even she shouldn't have to keep apologizing this endlessly.
changeReason: Changed from a general statement ('no reason anyone should') to a specific one about 'she' (彼女だって), more accurately reflecting the JPN subject.

TextENGNew: I felt a flicker of irritation at whoever it was who wouldn't forgive her.
No matter what mistake was made, there's nothing that can't be forgiven.
There's no such thing as a mistake you can't make right.
You just have to be more careful going forward.
...Still, she kept apologizing.
Then...had she really done something that couldn't be undone?
I didn't know what she'd done, but if it really couldn't be undone—all the more reason to forgive her.
No matter how many times she apologized, it wouldn't change a thing.
And still she continued to apologize in that heartbroken voice...
Hey. Whoever it is she keeps apologizing to.
Just go ahead and forgive her already.
She's apologizing...in such a wretched voice...
"Keiichi, we're almost there. Wake up."
My father gave me a nudge and I finally came up from my drowsiness.
changeReason: Changed 'nap' to 'drowsiness'; まどろみ means light dozing/drowsiness, distinct from a deliberate nap.

TextENGNew: It seemed the train had finally pulled into the last stop.
We'd transferred between the bullet train and local lines several times over the last few hours.
The view beyond the window made it hard to believe this was the same country—let alone the same era—as the city I'd been in half a day ago.
From here we'd take a car up into the mountain roads.
Then, where the thick trees pressing in on the mountain road suddenly opened up...
There was the place I now call home. Hinamizawa.
